And again, it's based on those patterns that we have that have laid down since childhood of what experts should sound like, what they look like, how they dress, all that stuff. That's in, again, the unconscious mind, meaning it's in the deepest recesses of your memory. Cause at any given point in time, as you move through the world, your conscious mind, let me just say, conscious, unconscious, okay? Your conscious mind has a very limited amount of processing power, it's limited. Right now you're listening to my voice, you're hanging on my every word, you're trying to think, okay, does it make sense, you're analyzing that? You see my face visually, maybe you notice something that's directly in front of you, if there's a loud noise in the background, a person sitting to your left or right, but other than that, guess what? You delete the rest of it just so you're like, okay, here's what's important, that's what my conscious awareness is focused on. That's the gift that your brain gives you because your conscious mind does not have enough processing power to see everything. It's not worried about this little plant here, or the wind on your skin, to the pressure of the seat on your ass right now, or the fact that you have to maintain the same heartbeat, same respiration, same core bi-temperature. Imagine if your conscious mind, okay, make sure my breath is the same, I gotta keep breathing the right way, while I listen to you, you couldn't do it. You know what I'm saying? We can't walk at your gum at the same time, while the exaggerated terms here, you know, you can't beat your heart at the same time as you can manage your digestive tract and listen to me at the same time. You know, you can't do it. The power is not there in your conscious mind. But your unconscious mind, that has literally unlimited processing power. The best metaphor is like the unconscious mind is like the cloud. It's like Amazon's cloud. There's unlimited storage capability there. Everything that you ever see here, and your whole life, it goes to the cloud, and your own personal cloud, in your unconscious mind. And that it's organized in a way without you thinking about it. You organize it into these patterns. You form patterns. Because what you do is you take all the stuff that your conscious mind can't handle. So watch, right now you have me in your field of vision, you listen to my voice, maybe one or two other things, right? The rest of the image, everything else around you, your five senses still take it in. But your brain says, okay, it's called the reticular activating system, body brain, it says, okay, that stuff's not important. I can let that stuff just fly past the conscious mind. I know the unconscious mind will capture it all, and it collates it, takes it, and organize it into these little patterns of behavior that allow you to move through the world. So every time you walk up to a door, you don't say strange door, what's that? What's that? Wooded handle, let me see that. Hmm, is that, is that a door? No, you're unconscious, like as you're approaching your door, your unconscious mind spits out, you hit a pattern for a door, and you also know that it's safe to walk through the door, it's not gonna be like an alternative universe, and say it's another room, because you've walked through 10,000 doors. So when your brain, your unconscious mind does, it generalizes things, forms these patterns, and when you approach your door, your eyes send that directly to the unconscious mind, it hits the pattern called doors in non-combat situations, so to speak, because if you're in Iraq right now, but you'd probably be approaching your door, like fucking, you ever see that happen? People will approach doors, Navy SEALs with fucking guns out, they're very different, they're like, turn it, is there a bomb? Because that's a different pattern. The logical mind uses words to build an airtight logical case. When you're moving someone down the straight line from the open to the close, you need to create both logical and emotional certainty. Well, for the most part, and again, nothing is always 100%, for the most part, logical certainty is created through the words that you say. While emotional certainty is created through a, something called pacing and leading, where you can actually use the tone of your voice to make someone more emotionally excited, but also it's based on the words you say, but how these words paint a picture. You wanna paint a picture. So when you're getting someone to an emotional, to certain levels, like you say, imagine yourself in the future using the product. So you actually paint a picture of them using words, but you imagine them to, in the future, feeling good, whatever pain they have been relieved, whatever they felt out of control about, they now feel back in control again. So it's still through words, but it's about words painting a picture versus words just saying, picking boxes. Like logical persuasion is about, check the box, check the box, check the box. This is a key point. When someone is really at a very high level of logical certainty, they can explain to someone else why it makes sense. But there's all these rules that you have to adhere to as you're doing that. So at the end of the day, they say, oh my God, yeah, yeah. A script is about creating an airtight logical case that is so compelling that it's, what you have is the best in class. It just makes fucking sense. It's awesome. It's gonna resolve their pain, right? It's gonna be the best value for the money. That's your core goal, right? He's gonna let me change my mind with it. He didn't reserve his best stuff, I wanna have some stuff in my arsenal here, so that when, and this is the looping, you were talking about, right? And what I do is when I make a presentation, what I do is I frame, I don't front load, you know? Imagine if you have like, if you have 12 core benefits, and remember, benefit always is attached to a feature, there's gotta be both, right? Without the feature is essentially the logical side, the benefits, the emotional side of the equation, right? If you just talk about, as people say, you'll be only self-benefits, that's not true. You have to mention the feature. So you mention a feature, and you expand on the benefit. The mistake people make is they will expand on the feature, that's that you don't wanna do that, but what you can't do is just go from benefit to benefit to benefit, you'll say, okay, well, how? I thought, oh, are you gonna get this, or are you gonna get that, or are you gonna get this? And you don't even mention what feature gives you that, people say, I don't understand, makes no sense to me. So it's not, don't fall for the trap, where you never mention features. You have to mention features, but you expand on the benefit, all right? So essentially, imagine there's a dozen of those things, right? And you give them all in that first presentation, you're like, what do you think? Sound good? They're like, let me think about it. You're like, nothing else, oh shit, like what do I say next? I'm gonna make you more certain, no, what I'd rather do is take my best three or four, or let's just say, well, the best is not even the right word. I'll take the three or four features that allow me to best paint the picture of what my product is looking. It's big picture, I frame. I'm not gonna start off and say, listen, when he started presenting, he did frame, he started off and gave me the frame, right? Because he knows what he's doing, but the point is he then started getting more intricate and more interesting. You would never stop and say, listen, let me tell you my program. What we do is in the morning, we have this feature, it's 8,000 hours, 8,000 hours of video course, like what the fuck are you talking about? 8,000 hours of video. How about start off and what are the core things you teach? You would never say, how many hours of video? How many quizzes we give you? That's small picture minutiae, you get it? You start off with big picture stuff, you frame a house. Like when you build a home, the first thing is you pour the foundation. So the question is, for Paul, what's the foundation of what he does? Well, the foundation is, we are the largest certification company in the world. That's the big picture foundation. We're the largest, fastest, and the most well respected in the entire region. That's his foundation, right? Big picture stuff. Not like, oh, let me tell you, we have these 12 courses. No, that comes later on. So what you have to do here is when you, but you first write the whole thing out in one giant pattern, and then you say, okay, this is your number of big picture, small picture, and you start actually breaking it up yourself. The first step is to write it all out, because then you're gonna find out, okay, we're not gonna front load, we're gonna frame. Frame again means that we use the parts of our thesis. So remember, you have a thesis you wrote out, the whole thesis, which is the kitchen sink about your business. Then you get a hat, that's your Bible, and then you get a pull from that to write your script. You first write the thesis, then you pull from that thread script. So you say, okay, well, what is my best framing argument here that explain what I do, that sort of set me up that to fill in the brackets. More about, it's more about the what, and your loops are more about the how and why. There's the, the secret is how, why does it work so well? That's in a loop, that won't be in your initial pattern. Why it works so well. Exactly how it works. Those are your secondary patterns. It's what you offer. It's this, and this gives you that. That's your opening patterns. First we do this, we do this and the reason why we're so great at it, that comes later on. That's, oh, but the true beauty is, and the reason we're so great is because we also have, and you have to dig deeper into that, okay? So you never would want to front load a script. We would give everything up front, and then you have nothing left to say. People tune out number one, because it's just too much, they get overwhelmed, they get bored with tears, and you have not to change their mind with it, if they say no. So that's number one. Number two, okay, is that each word is crucial. So the prom I see more than, this is the number one problem with all scripts, the quality, the biggest call center out there, major call center guy, right? Is that the scripts that you write are not really usable. You write scripts, you hand to your sales force, and they're like toilet paper, because they're not actually usable scripts. Meaning that if you were to read from this script word for word, you'd sound like an idiot. You'd sound like you're like, hi, my name is Jordan Belfort. In other words, the reason many people are negatively anchored towards scripts is because they've gotten, people have handed them poor scripts. When you're handed a real straight line script, that script can actually be read word for word if you're on the phone, or the patterns are memorized when you're, you know, the core patterns when you're in person, right? The point is, the words are written in such a way as if they're totally natural speech. The right number of contractions, you know, in the movie, The Wolf of Wall Street, Leo goes, you know, hey, you know, the name of the company is Aerotime International. He says, name of the company? The word the is dropped out, and the word is is dropped out. In his script, those words were not there. It was name of the company comma, Aerotime International. It is a cutting edge, and it was not contracted. It is for power. So those, when you write a script, every single word is crucial, because if the words aren't exactly right, it's not usable. And as soon as that happens, you know what they do, they throw away the script. This is why it's so, the big reason why scripts don't get adopted, because if you imagine this, think about it logically, guys. Just step back for a second, right? I'm gonna slow down my speech, just get, because I'm very passionate about this, but let's think about it logically, okay? Why wouldn't someone use a script? If someone can hand you the perfect thing to say, that would, imagine the best possible way to present something and overcome objections, and just the perfect, if someone just spent a week just sitting down and coming to the perfect thing, proven to work, why wouldn't you use it every time? What was that? Well, no, I'll tell you why, no. Why would you use it, because they don't give you that. That's not what most people hand you. What they hand you is something that's not actually usable. They spend a week doing it, but it's not a real straight line script. In other words, it would say the name of the company is Aerotron International, and then when you try to use that, people would not listen to you, because you sound wooden, and you don't sound authentic. There's all these things that you must do in the script. If you don't sound authentic, you're done. A script is worthless, and salespeople are smart to know, intuitively, they say, you know what, I'm not using this shit. I couldn't use it, I want to use the script, it doesn't sound right, it doesn't work, why would I fuck? And you say, people want this, you know what, because they probably, most people have been handed scripts that make no sense, I'm not using this shit, but if I wrote a script the right way, and I hit, when I write a script, the language patterns I use are gonna be exactly as I would really say them out in the field or in person. I'd have all the missing words, the way humans speak. You have this, when I speak into a camera, if I was to get this, and I get them a little transcribed, I'm like, what the fuck does that mean? When you transcribe someone's natural speech, it makes no sense, right, you know, it makes no sense, but yet it made perfect sense to the audience, like, oh yeah, everyone's like, yeah, right, right, because spoken word and the written word are not the same. And the mistake people make with fucking scripts is they use the written word and not the spoken word. That's the big miss here, and because of that, the salespeople says, why can't they get it, and they say, guys, you gotta use the script, they're like, they'll try it once or twice, and it stops, it doesn't work. I'd rather just use my own shit because this doesn't make any sense. But a real straight line script, every single word has been poured over meticulously and been fashioned to sound just right. The right number of beats, I mean, examples, right number of beats are in each sentence. How many syllables, how many beats? You know when I write a script, I am like, a lot of it's about how many beats are in a certain sentence. I'll change words if the right number of beats are there because there's a certain way, a rhythm of speaking that just sounds great, and when you speak naturally, you will usually, people who speak well naturally, they have a certain number of beats in each word, and they'll speed up or slow down to make those beats sound good. You know when you're in the car and the radio's on, if a song comes on the radio and it's out of beat, it's like, what do you do? Change your fucking channel. But when, something is that, you know, I just watched this movie, this Elton John movie, I don't know, you guys gotta see this movie's fucking phenomenal, Elton John, right? It's called The Rocket Man, it's really worth seeing, it's fucking great. But every, the melody, the words, it's, when you're in the presence of that greatness, when someone can communicate through music, right? The reason people are hooked on the music is there's a certain number of words, certain number of beats, certain melody, well in sales, what's your melody? It's your tonality. That's the equivalent, your sales, the way your tonality is a melody, does it sound pleasing to the ear? That little mm-hmm, okay, is keeping them in the game and developing something called consistency. Now, this is on a very low list, so consistently, right, just to write it down for you guys, consistency. So consistency means that people want to remain consistent with things they've said, actions they've taken. See, does it make sense? Yes, yes, yes, yes, they're in the habit of saying yes. And just so you know, this is the lowest level of consistency. It's not like, it's not that powerful, it matters, but it's not gonna make people, oh, I said yes 10 times, okay, I'll bar, okay, I'll bar, no, that's not that. It just keeps them moving up, makes sense, uh-huh, I gotcha, uh-huh, gotcha, all right, you wanna get them in the habit of saying, uh-huh, yes, huh, not that it's gonna cause them to say yes in the end, but you're developing just that rhythm, uh-huh, they agree with you, you're moving them down the line, slowly but surely. But there are types of consistency in sales that are incredibly powerful. For instance, when you're looping, that's when consistency really starts to matter. When you are in the back end of the sale, and you say this is the idea, it makes sense, you say, yeah, it sounds good, then you loop back, they're like, oh no, yeah, I really like the idea. Now, yeah, I got it, that's when the, so now they say I love the idea, and then the next pattern, if you knew me and trusted me, you'd probably be buying it, and I say yes, I would, they say yes, that's true, so yes, I love your product, yes, if I knew you, I would, the reason I'm not buying, I don't know, you're right, I'm skeptical, I don't know, you don't trust you, right? If you knew what you're buying, yes, I would, check, now, if you could actually get them, if you could overcome this lack of trust somehow, right? You start to put a person, a little bit of a logic box, because they just said, I love your product, I'm not buying it, I don't know you. But you said, if you knew what I could do, if you could trust, you'd be buying it, and say yeah, well then I would, you say exactly. Now that, I can understand, you don't know me, I don't have a track record, so they're feeling pretty good, because then you say, yeah, I don't trust you, right? I say, let me show you why you should trust me. And then you say to them, you go through this whole pattern, right, all of a sudden now, you, essentially they said, yep, if I trusted you, I'd buy, I love your product, so remember, these two things happen separate from each other. When you start, when you run your first loop, it's like, you know, it's like, I hear what you're saying, just doesn't the idea itself make sense to you? Do you like the idea? I'm not saying, well, I'm not saying, do you like, do you trust me, it's all I'm saying, it's nothing matters, just all I wanna know is does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? I have my yes for my first 10 and one silo, right? When I get to the second 10, a yes for me and the second silo, all of a sudden now, it starts to create some real consistency, it's like, shit, first if I knew you I'd be buying, and then all of a sudden they say, you had to trust you now, that's when consistency starts to really make sense, because they say, if I trusted you, I'd buy, and then you say things to make them trust you, and you show them why they should trust you, and if you're in rapport then, and they'll be like, wow, this guy is trustworthy, so now, they're still saying, what's my reason now not to buy, I just said I'd buy if I trusted you. So that's when consistency starts to come into play. You really believe you're ready to take action, just come to the front just to show, great, great, okay? Now, for those of you guys, I'll tell you what, I'll take 10% off, go to the back of the room, and you'll get 10 times as many to go, because they first raised their hand, they stood up, they said, yes, I'm interested, I want it, now you say, go to the back of the room, and they run to the back of the room. That's how you create consistency in a seminar room, right? Verse saying, guys, if you're interested, go to the back of the room right now, only a few will go, but once they raise their hand, stand up, come to the front, they've now taken actions, they want to remain consistent with those actions. You've seen this, you've spoken right, you've seen a lot of, that's how it works, you get people to say, yes, I like it, stand up, and by doing that, they will go to the back, and the results, by the way, are not even close to being the same. If you just try to get someone to go to the back of the room without first then making public declarations about it, only a few will go. Once people declare publicly, they want to remain consistent, okay, so that's what we're always doing, as we're moving someone down the straight line. Next, right? Using transitional words and phrases. So on some level, this is similar to what, that each word being crucial, but what it's about is that there's certain words like now, well, say now, Jim, the reason for the call, you have these transitional words that allow you to go from one thought to the next, and without those words, what happens to your script just doesn't make as much sense, and it sounds wrong. So let's say I want to go from a state of, I'm told that I'm very certain, like now, it does this, does that, does that, and now I want to create scarcity. I don't say, I just, I wouldn't go and just start talking about this only, Jim, there's only five of these, I say now, and I use my transition of now, there's only five of these left. So you have these words that you use in scripts that allow you to stop, completely change your tonality, lower your voice or raise your voice, because without, I'm telling you, out of those words, the script won't make sense. You can't just say, this is that, there's only five of these left, you need something in between that allows you to catch your breath, shift your tonality. Now, why is that important? Again, what happens is if you write scripts, then it'll lack me, you don't have to say, oh, it doesn't work. I sound stupid. I can't use the script, it's not real, because in a real conversation, you use transitional words. So just be aware of that. Next, big one, painting a powerful picture. The biggest mistake people make when they write scripts is that they don't use stories to illustrate their points. Storytelling is probably, is the oldest form of communication. The Bible, right? The greatest story ever told, right? Since people sat around campfires back in the early, even in the end of the full genes, they were saying, that was how people communicated. They would tell stories. The human brain is wired in such a way that we learn through stories. A perfect example is when I wrote this first script for Eastman Kodak, right? And that just blows, there's this amazingly effective script. There's many ways to explain why Eastman Kodak was a great buy. Many ways, right? One way is like, listen, the company's earning four dollars a share right now. The entire consumer products industry is trading at 25 times earnings. Do the math, four times 25 is $100. Right now the stock's 42. It's a fact, right? It's undervalued, right? Next, break up value. Take up the company, break it into small chunks, self-divisions, 130 hour break up value. Stock's 42, right? Book value alone of the company with cash. Company's trading at only three times its cash reserves. The rest of the industry trades 10 times its cash reserves. And the point was they were being sued by Polaroid. That for patent infringement, so the price had been depressed. You get it? So you were making all these, your eye was, listen, the company's in a loss of a Polaroid and the stock is really cheap right now. Look, check out the metrics. The entire industry is this. It's trading here, right? That's how you explain it, right? And if you did that, no one would buy from you. That's just a very logical, that's not why they would buy. So how we get into buying was like this. Next, creating urgency at the end. This is a huge one, huge, huge. There's logical reasons to buy and there's emotional reasons to buy. And they're both powerful. So I said, let me try Harry's Blades, you know, I'll try Harry's, right? I go to Harry's Blades, right, and they're really good though, and I go on Amazon, because I know, because they do a bet, it's one click buy, right? I go on Amazon, and I get to that thing, it's like 47 different fucking Blades, eight different, I'm like, ah, fuck, you just give me a shit, and I went out and had my assistant give me a shit razor with Blades, because it was easy. The mistake people make, you do everything right in your presentation, you do everything great, and then when it comes time to ask for the order, you say, you know, here's how you get started, I want you to go down to the post office, get a self-addressed stamped envelope, okay, fill it out, make a picture of your ID, put that in there, go out, and then jump through a hoop of fire, throw up your left testicle, send it to me, and you're like 82 fucking steps, and then you can get started, get the product, versus getting started is so simple, question your name, some basic information, we do everything else for you, and believe me, you'll be glad you did. Amazon has it to a science, where they just say, you see it, click, you own it, you get the fucking, it's like a magic, they got it next to an hour, it's like how the fuck can you do that, right? But they make it so simple, my card's on file, is why iTunes, my card's on file, I can buy shit easily, but every, listen, in reality, there's sometimes there are, some industries, like insurance, there's something they have to do, but the point is when you're explaining how to get started, what you're telling us, we make it as easy as possible for you, we take out as much of the guesswork for you, it's why companies say batteries included, no assembly required, every father's worst nightmare, guess what, at least my type of guy, I couldn't put shit together, there's toys, I bought my kids a toy, I'm like really cool fucking, contractors to come over to fucking put the toy in, I'm like going to the hospital, I'll pay you a million bucks just to put together this fucking, this toy, I couldn't, and my kids get toys with 80 pieces, I'm like, oh my God, right? Give me the toys fully assembled, with the batteries inside, that's the one I want, I'll pay extra for that one, we always want to buy stuff, easy, because why, because the energy that we have to put in to get a benefit, right, watch me just draw it out for you, we'll come to a close here, right? So the energy in of the equation means how much energy, it's like equal, MC squared, this is my fucking Einstein's equation, right? Fuck that, I got one better for you, okay? Mine doesn't blow up fucking countries, okay? Energy, E, energy in, how much energy do I have to put in, in the terms of number one, money, money is stored energy, I work hard, I get money for that, I deploy something to pay my bills to live every day, and the excess money goes what? In the bank, it's stored energy for my hard work, I can then deploy that, I can unleash that energy as I want to buy things, but that's stored energy, money, which is why you say it's only this, which is why when someone says to you, how much does it cost? And you say, oh, it's $9.99, it's only $1,000. You never would do that, it's so, it's how much does it cost, which is a buy signal, right? I say, oh, it's only $1,000, and let me tell, remind you what you get, benefit one, you get this, this, that, you get this, that, and believe me, you'll be glad you did this. I always stack on the benefits to remind them that this is the outcome they're getting, and the very little energy they have to expend to get it. If you set up a closing scenario that where they have to expend energy in the form of money, time, filling shit out, putting in their credit, that's all energy expenditure, and you get this benefit, you're not gonna buy. Everything else could be in, it's this last second check that the brain runs, is that, okay, everything is great, I love everything, but how much energy do I have to put into it to get that benefit? You want to, it's no big deal, very simple, okay, we do everything, believe me, you'll be energy in, low, massive benefits out, people tend to buy. My next question, that's a good answer. Next question is from Patrick Solonius. During the intelligence gathering stage, what questions will expose the prospects' attitudes towards salespeople their action threshold slash their action threshold? There's really no way to find someone's action threshold because sometimes people sound really tougher than just not and vice versa. The action threshold exposes itself when you're in the back half of the sale and you've got them sounding really certain, but they're still not buying. That's when it reveals it. That's the only surefire way to know. In terms of what questions to use, it's not supposed to reveal someone's action threshold, but to reveal their attitudes towards salespeople in general, it'll be like, so what type of, how your experience has been in the past with similar products? And typically when you ask about the products, they will then talk about the product and the people who sold them because they're very much more than the same. They say, I don't know about this company, they're bad customers, the guy was a lawyer, or you guess you might, it's really more about asking things about what are your past relationships? Reframing fear, understanding what fear really means, right? You know, fear, if you're all fear means, it's telling you that you need to have heightened awareness. It's just, you can't take sales personally. It's the biggest mistake you make. Every, you start a little bit this way. Every time, if you know, if you're keeping track of your sales funnel and you know you make 200 calls a day or 100, wherever that number is, right? And 90% say no. And 10% say yes. Yes. Then you know what every yes is worth, right? But you also know what every no is worth. Because you just, bam, thank you. Just maybe four dollars. So you have an average. Once you know what your average is, every call is a profitable call where they say yes or no, because you're one step closer to your fucking, yes, it's a numbers game. Sales is a numbers game. So when someone says no, but you know your numbers, it's like, boom, I got, you're getting 50 cents for every no on some level. You follow what I'm saying? Cause you know, once you know what your numbers are, it's just, it's part of the game. Whether they say yes or no, it's all gonna wash out. Like, you know, you can roll the dice 20 times and get 20 heads. But if you're rolling a thousand, you're gonna get even. Think of it like that. It's a numbers game. The more times you dial the phone, the more closes you to get, the nose are as good as the yeses, cause they get you one step closer to a yes. That's the way it works. All right.
